key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr/Ni eutectic interface plus nanoscale lamellar confinement enables coordinated plastic co-deformation, allowing a normally brittle hard phase to sustain slip and twinning without cracking.
engineering_relevance: This suggests interface and spacing engineering in ultrafine eutectics can raise strength while preserving ductility for additively manufactured structural alloys.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion created a lamellar Cr-rich BCC / Ni-rich FCC eutectic with ~450 nm spacing.
Atomic-resolution STEM/HR-STEM mapped the stepped Kurdjumov–Sachs interface and Ni segregation near the BCC side.
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension tracked deformation; atomistic modeling probed interface-controlled slip activation.
evidence_signal: Correlative microscopy and mechanical testing show high flow stress with large plastic strains and no cracking, alongside a clear deformation sequence from interfacial dislocation pile-up to twinning, shear, and mobile dislocations in BCC.
watch_item: The observed co-deformation may depend sensitively on precise interlamellar spacing, interface coherency/stepping, and local chemistry, so transferability to other processing routes or compositions may be limited.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
